question: Tienes experiencia con JMS. ¿Cómo lo compararías con Kafka?
interviewerIntent: Evaluar los fundamentos de mensajería, la honestidad y la capacidad de transferir experiencia sin afirmar que son idénticos.
shortAnswer: Mi experiencia con JMS me da bases sólidas en mensajería asíncrona: productores, consumidores, reintentos, DLQ e idempotencia. Kafka añade un modelo diferente: un log de solo anexión (append-only log), offsets gestionados por el consumidor, paralelismo basado en particiones y replay de mensajes. Los patrones se transfieren, pero el modelo operativo es fundamentalmente diferente.
modelAnswer:
Contexto: Tengo experiencia en producción con JMS y ActiveMQ Artemis para la integración asíncrona entre servicios.

Conceptos transferibles: Productores y consumidores de mensajes, desacoplamiento asíncrono, reintentos ante fallos, manejo de mensajes fallidos (dead-letter), procesamiento idempotente y arquitectura orientada a eventos.

Diferencias clave que reconozco:
- JMS: mensaje consumido = eliminado de la cola. Kafka: los mensajes se retienen en el log (replayable).
- JMS: el broker rastrea el estado de entrega. Kafka: el consumidor gestiona su propio offset (posición).
- JMS: cola = punto a punto. Kafka: tópico + consumer groups = múltiples lectores independientes de los mismos datos.
- JMS: orden dentro de una cola. Kafka: orden dentro de una partición (no entre particiones).
- JMS: confirmación (ACK) por mensaje. Kafka: commit de offset (puede ser por lotes).

Mi enfoque: Transferiría mis patrones de mensajería (consumidores idempotentes, DLQ, backoff de reintentos) mientras aprendo las operaciones específicas de Kafka (partition keys, rebalanceo de consumer groups, gestión de offsets, evolución de esquemas).

Compromiso (Trade-off): Kafka introduce una complejidad operativa (ZooKeeper/KRaft, gestión de particiones, políticas de retención) que JMS no tiene, pero permite event sourcing y replay que JMS no facilita.
senaiExample: SGN3 utiliza más de 80 colas JMS en Artemis para procesamiento asíncrono: eventos de matrícula, notificaciones, integraciones. Los MDBs consumen con reintentos y DLQ. Estos patrones se transfieren a @KafkaListener.
reweExample: El equipo TRAB publica eventos logísticos (DeliveryPlanned, TruckDeparted, DeliveryCompleted) en tópicos de Kafka. Múltiples servicios consumen de forma independiente: seguimiento, analítica, notificaciones a conductores.
keyPoints: Reconocer patrones transferibles (productor/consumidor, reintentos, DLQ, idempotencia)
Explicar diferencias clave (log vs cola, offset vs ack, retención vs eliminación)
NO afirmar que Kafka es simplemente otra cola
Mostrar disposición para aprender aspectos operativos
Conectar con eventos logísticos reales de REWE
mistakesToAvoid: Decir que JMS y Kafka son lo mismo
Afirmar tener experiencia en producción con Kafka si no es así
Ignorar la diferencia fundamental de modelo (log vs cola)
No mencionar lo que necesitas aprender

followUps.question: ¿Cuál es el mayor error al pasar de JMS a Kafka?
followUps.answerHint: Tratar a Kafka como una cola, esperando que los mensajes desaparezcan tras el consumo. También: no pensar en las partition keys para el ordenamiento.

vocabulary.meaning: posición del consumidor en el log; equivalente a un marcador de libro
vocabulary.example: El consumidor hace commit del offset 42, lo que significa que ha procesado todos los mensajes hasta esa posición en la partición.

vocabulary.meaning: grupo de consumidores que se reparten las particiones entre sí
vocabulary.example: Tres instancias de un servicio en el mismo consumer group tienen asignadas particiones diferentes; el trabajo se distribuye.

question: ¿Cuándo usarías Kafka en lugar de una llamada REST directa entre servicios?
interviewerIntent: Probar la comprensión de los compromisos entre comunicación síncrona y asíncrona.
shortAnswer: Usa Kafka cuando: el productor no deba esperar al consumidor, múltiples servicios necesiten el mismo evento, necesites reintentos/resiliencia, el orden importe o quieras repetir (replay) eventos. Usa REST cuando necesites una respuesta inmediata o la interacción sea de tipo solicitud-respuesta.
modelAnswer:
Contexto: En sistemas distribuidos, los servicios se comunican de forma síncrona (REST) o asíncrona (Kafka).

Usa Kafka cuando:
1. Disparar y olvidar (Fire-and-forget): el productor no necesita respuesta (evento DeliveryPlanned).
2. Fan-out: múltiples servicios necesitan el mismo evento (seguimiento + analítica + notificaciones).
3. Resiliencia: el consumidor está caído temporalmente; los mensajes esperan en el log.
4. Orden: los eventos de la misma entrega deben procesarse en secuencia.
5. Replay: un nuevo servicio necesita procesar eventos históricos.

Usa REST cuando:
1. Solicitud-respuesta: necesitas una respuesta ahora (ej. consultar disponibilidad de stock).
2. Consulta simple: GET del estado actual de una entrega.
3. Baja latencia: respuesta en menos de 100ms.

En mi experiencia con JMS, tomaba decisiones similares: JMS para asíncrono/fire-and-forget, REST para respuestas inmediatas. Kafka añade el fan-out y el replay que JMS no ofrece fácilmente.
senaiExample: SGN3: REST para consultas en tiempo real (estado de matrícula). JMS para procesos asíncronos (generar certificado, enviar email). El mismo marco de decisión aplica a Kafka.
reweExample: REST: el conductor consulta detalles de la entrega. Kafka: el evento DeliveryPlanned es consumido por el servicio de tracking, el optimizador de rutas y analítica de forma independiente.
keyPoints: Kafka = asíncrono, fire-and-forget, fan-out, resiliencia
REST = síncrono, request-reply, respuesta inmediata
Fan-out es la característica estrella de Kafka sobre las colas JMS
Misma lógica de decisión que usé con JMS vs REST en Java EE

mission: Ordena el **flujo de eventos de Kafka** para una entrega que está siendo planificada.

items.label: Kafka asigna el evento a una partición (por ID de entrega)
El Broker lo anexa al log de la partición (persistencia)
TrackingService lee el evento en su offset actual
El consumidor procesa y hace commit del offset

explain: Productor → partición (por clave) → el broker almacena (log de solo anexión) → el consumidor lee en su offset → procesa y confirma. Idea clave: el mensaje permanece en el log DESPUÉS del consumo (a diferencia de JMS). Otros consumidores aún pueden leerlo.

mission: Publicas eventos de entrega en Kafka. ¿Cuál debería ser la **partition key**?

options.label: ID de Entrega (Delivery ID)
options.desc: Todos los eventos de la misma entrega van a la misma partición.

options.label: ID de Conductor (Driver ID)
options.desc: Todos los eventos del mismo conductor van a la misma partición.

options.label: Sin clave (round-robin)
options.desc: Eventos distribuidos uniformemente para máximo rendimiento.

pros: Eventos de la misma entrega están ORDENADOS
El consumidor ve planned→dispatched→delivered en secuencia
La idempotencia por entrega es más simple
a.verdict: Es la mejor opción para eventos logísticos. Necesitas orden por entrega.

nuance: Partition key = garantía de orden. Misma clave → misma partición → ordenado. En logística, los eventos del ciclo de vida de una entrega DEBEN estar ordenados.

mission: Un consumidor de Kafka falla al procesar un evento de entrega. **¿Qué debería pasar?**

options.label: Reintentar inmediatamente, bloquear la partición
options.desc: Reintentar el mismo mensaje hasta que tenga éxito o intervención manual.

options.label: Reintentar con backoff, luego Dead Letter Topic
options.desc: Reintentar 3 veces con retroceso exponencial. Si sigue fallando, enviar a DLT y continuar.

options.label: Omitir y registrar (skip and log)
options.desc: Registrar el error, hacer commit del offset y pasar al siguiente mensaje.

pros: Los mensajes en DLT pueden ser investigados
b.verdict: Es el estándar de la industria. Mismo patrón que max-delivery-attempts + DLQ en JMS.

nuance: Esto es idéntico a lo que configuraba con JMS/Artemis. El patrón es el mismo, solo cambia la API.

mission: Traduce este **MDB de JMS** a un consumidor de Spring Kafka.
explain: @MessageDriven → @KafkaListener. Cola JMS → tópico Kafka + consumer group. La lógica de procesamiento es IDENTICA.

mission: Compare: JMS Acknowledgment vs Kafka Offset Commit

conceptA.name: JMS Acknowledgment
conceptA.definition: Consumer acknowledges each message. Acknowledged = removed from queue. Not acknowledged = redelivered. Broker tracks state per message.

conceptB.name: Kafka Offset Commit
conceptB.definition: Consumer commits its position (offset) in the partition. Message stays in log regardless. Committing = "I processed up to here." Broker stores offset per consumer group.

keyDifference: JMS: per-message acknowledgment, message removed after ack. Kafka: positional bookmark (offset), message stays forever (until retention expires). Kafka allows replay by resetting offset; JMS does not.
javaExample: JMS: message.acknowledge() removes it. Kafka: consumer.commitSync() says "I'm at offset 42" — the message at 42 stays in the topic and other consumers can still read it.
interviewAnswer: In JMS, acknowledging removes the message. In Kafka, committing an offset is a bookmark — the message stays in the log. This enables replay: a new consumer can start from offset 0 and reprocess everything. It also means multiple consumer groups read the same messages independently, which is impossible with JMS queues.
answer:
**JMS Acknowledgment:** Consumer acknowledges each message. Acknowledged = removed from queue. Not acknowledged = redelivered. Broker tracks state per message.

**Kafka Offset Commit:** Consumer commits its position (offset) in the partition. Message stays in log regardless. Committing = "I processed up to here." Broker stores offset per consumer group.


**Key Difference:** JMS: per-message acknowledgment, message removed after ack. Kafka: positional bookmark (offset), message stays forever (until retention expires). Kafka allows replay by resetting offset; JMS does not.

question: ¿Cómo aseguras que un consumidor de Kafka sea idempotente?
interviewerIntent: Probar la comprensión de la entrega 'at-least-once' y cómo manejar duplicados.
shortAnswer: Kafka proporciona entrega 'at-least-once' por defecto: el mismo mensaje puede entregarse varias veces (ej. si el consumidor cae tras procesar pero antes del commit). Mi consumidor debe manejar duplicados usando un ID de evento único almacenado en una tabla de desduplicación, verificado atómicamente antes de procesar.
modelAnswer:
Contexto: Kafka garantiza 'at-least-once' por defecto. Si el consumidor procesa pero falla antes de confirmar el offset, el mensaje se reentrega al reiniciar.

Solución: Cada evento lleva un eventId único. Antes de procesar, verifico si existe en una tabla `processed_events` mediante: `INSERT ... ON CONFLICT (event_id) DO NOTHING`. Si el insert no afecta filas, es un duplicado y se ignora.

Conexión JMS: Usábamos exactamente el mismo patrón con los MDBs de Artemis. El concepto se transfiere perfectamente.
senaiExample: Los MDBs de SGN3 usan INSERT ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING con una columna chave_idempotencia. Mismo patrón, diferente framework.

mission: Completa el consumidor de Spring Kafka que escucha **eventos de entrega**.
answer: KafkaListener
explain: @KafkaListener es el equivalente en Spring Kafka a @MessageDriven (JMS).

mission: Complete the event publisher that sends to a Kafka topic.
answer: send
explain: KafkaTemplate.send(topic, key, value) publishes a message. The key (delivery ID) determines the partition — all events for the same delivery go to the same partition, preserving order. Equivalent to JMS messageProducer.send() but with an explicit key for partitioning.

mission: Which statement about Kafka is FALSE?

snippets.code:
Messages in Kafka are retained
after consumption. Consumers
can replay by resetting offset.

Kafka guarantees ordering
across ALL partitions in a
topic. Events are always
globally ordered.

Multiple consumer groups can
read the same topic independently.
Each group tracks its own offset.

answer: b
explain: FALSE: Kafka guarantees ordering only WITHIN a partition, NOT across partitions. If a topic has 4 partitions, events in different partitions may be consumed out of order relative to each other. To guarantee ordering for a delivery, use the delivery ID as partition key → same partition → ordered.

mission: El evento DeliveryPlanned necesita un **nuevo campo** (estimatedArrival). ¿Cómo evolucionas el esquema?

options.label: Añadir el campo como opcional (compatible hacia atrás)
options.desc: El nuevo campo es opcional. Consumidores antiguos lo ignoran; los nuevos lo usan.

options.label: Crear una nueva versión del evento (V2)
options.desc: Publicar V1 y V2 durante la migración.

pros: Compatible hacia atrás: los consumidores antiguos no se rompen
a.verdict: Es el enfoque estándar para cambios no disruptivos. Añadir campos opcionales, nunca eliminar ni renombrar existentes.

scenario: The interviewer probes operational knowledge about Kafka in production.

questions.q: What happens when a consumer instance dies?
questions.hint: Consumer group rebalance — its partitions are redistributed to remaining instances. Messages are reprocessed from last committed offset (hence need for idempotency).

questions.q: How does scaling Kafka consumers work?
questions.hint: Add more consumer instances to the group (max = number of partitions). If 4 partitions and 4 instances → each gets 1. If 5 instances → one is idle.
Difference between latest produced offset and consumer committed offset. Growing lag = consumer is falling behind. Critical metric to monitor.

questions.q: How would you investigate a consumer that is falling behind?
questions.hint: Check consumer lag metric. Look at processing time per message. Check if external dependencies (DB, REST) are slow. Consider adding partitions + instances for parallelism.

questions.q: What happens if a topic runs out of disk space?
questions.hint: Messages are deleted by retention policy (time or size). If retention is too long and volume high, disk fills. Solution: tune retention, add brokers, monitor disk usage.

question: The interviewer probes operational knowledge about Kafka in production.
answer:
**Q: What happens when a consumer instance dies?**
Consumer group rebalance — its partitions are redistributed to remaining instances. Messages are reprocessed from last committed offset (hence need for idempotency).

**Q: How does scaling Kafka consumers work?**
Add more consumer instances to the group (max = number of partitions). If 4 partitions and 4 instances → each gets 1. If 5 instances → one is idle.

**Q: What is consumer lag?**
Difference between latest produced offset and consumer committed offset. Growing lag = consumer is falling behind. Critical metric to monitor.

**Q: How would you investigate a consumer that is falling behind?**
Check consumer lag metric. Look at processing time per message. Check if external dependencies (DB, REST) are slow. Consider adding partitions + instances for parallelism.

**Q: What happens if a topic runs out of disk space?**
Messages are deleted by retention policy (time or size). If retention is too long and volume high, disk fills. Solution: tune retention, add brokers, monitor disk usage.

question: ¿Qué eventos diseñarías para un sistema de seguimiento de entregas?
interviewerIntent: Evaluar la capacidad de pensar en eventos para el dominio logístico.
shortAnswer: Eventos del ciclo de vida: DeliveryPlanned, TruckDeparted, DeliveryInTransit, DeliveryCompleted, DeliveryFailed, DeliveryDelayed. Cada uno lleva el Delivery ID como clave (para el orden) y un payload relevante.
modelAnswer:
Diseñaría eventos basados en hitos de negocio: DeliveryPlanned (con ruta y conductor), TruckDeparted (salida de almacén), DeliveryInTransit (actualizaciones GPS), StopCompleted (firma de entrega) y DeliveryCompleted. 

Decisiones de diseño:
- Clave: Delivery ID para garantizar el orden por entrega.
- Tópico: Un tópico `delivery-events` con un campo de tipo de evento.
- Payload: Autocontenido (el consumidor no debería necesitar llamar al productor para obtener detalles).
- Timestamp: Cuándo ocurrió el hecho, no cuándo se publicó.
reweExample: En TRAB, exactamente estos eventos fluyen entre los sistemas de almacén, las apps de conductores y los planificadores.

mission: Compare: Event-Driven Architecture vs Event Sourcing

conceptA.name: Event-Driven Architecture
conceptA.definition: Services communicate via events. Producer publishes what happened. Consumer reacts. Current state stored in DB. Events are notifications.

conceptB.name: Event Sourcing
conceptB.definition: State IS the sequence of events. No separate DB for current state — rebuild state by replaying events from the beginning. Events are the source of truth.

keyDifference: Event-driven: events are communication between services (can be lost, DB is source of truth). Event sourcing: events ARE the data (replay to rebuild state). Most systems use event-driven without event sourcing.
javaExample: Event-driven: save delivery to DB, then publish DeliveryPlanned event for other services. Event sourcing: store DeliveryPlanned, TruckDeparted, DeliveryCompleted events — derive current delivery state by replaying them.
interviewAnswer: Most logistics systems use event-driven architecture — services communicate via Kafka events, but each service maintains its own database as source of truth. Event sourcing (state derived from events) is powerful for audit trails but adds complexity. I would start with event-driven and consider event sourcing only for domains where the complete history IS the business value.
answer:
**Event-Driven Architecture:** Services communicate via events. Producer publishes what happened. Consumer reacts. Current state stored in DB. Events are notifications.

**Event Sourcing:** State IS the sequence of events. No separate DB for current state — rebuild state by replaying events from the beginning. Events are the source of truth.


**Key Difference:** Event-driven: events are communication between services (can be lost, DB is source of truth). Event sourcing: events ARE the data (replay to rebuild state). Most systems use event-driven without event sourcing.

question: Tu tópico de eventos lleva 6 meses en producción con el esquema V1. Necesitas añadir un campo 'priority'. Hay 3 servicios suscritos. ¿Cómo evolucionas el esquema de forma segura?

options.label: A) Añadir el campo como opcional con un valor por defecto
options.description: Los nuevos eventos llevan prioridad; los antiguos se deserializan con el valor por defecto. No requiere cambios inmediatos en los consumidores.

options.label: B) Crear un nuevo tópico v2
options.description: Migrar productores y consumidores poco a poco.

explanation: La opción A es el estándar: añadir campos opcionales con valores por defecto mantiene la compatibilidad hacia atrás. El Schema Registry de Confluent (Avro/Protobuf) ayuda a validar esto antes de registrar el nuevo esquema.

question: Explica la diferencia entre at-most-once, at-least-once y exactly-once. ¿Cuándo usarías cada una?
modelAnswer: At-most-once: Se puede perder el mensaje, nunca se duplica (commit antes de procesar). Útil para logs no críticos. At-least-once: Nunca se pierde, pero puede haber duplicados (commit tras procesar). Es el estándar para el 95% de los casos de negocio, requiriendo consumidores idempotentes. Exactly-once: Sin pérdidas ni duplicados mediante transacciones de Kafka. Realidad práctica: Exactly-once solo funciona de Kafka a Kafka; si escribes en una BD externa, necesitas manejar la idempotencia a nivel de aplicación.

question: How do you monitor Kafka consumers in production? What metrics indicate problems?
modelAnswer:
**Key metrics:**

| Metric | Healthy | Alert threshold | Meaning |
|--------|---------|-----------------|---------|
| Consumer lag | <100 | >10,000 for 5min | Consumer falling behind producer |
| Rebalance frequency | Rare | >3/hour | Consumers crashing/restarting |
| Processing time/msg | <100ms | p99 >5s | Consumer logic is slow |
| Error rate | 0% | >1% | Deserialization or processing failures |
| DLT messages | 0 | >0 | Poison messages accumulating |

**Consumer lag** is the most critical:
```
Lag = Latest offset (what producer wrote) - Committed offset (what consumer processed)
```

**Monitoring tools:**
- Kafka CLI: `kafka-consumer-groups.sh --describe --group trab-delivery-service`
- Prometheus + kafka_consumer_lag_records gauge
- Grafana dashboard with lag per partition

**Alert response:**
- Lag growing slowly → consumer is slower than producer (optimize processing)
- Lag spike after deploy → new version has a bug or is slower
- Lag growing + consumer crashed → check logs, restart consumer group
- Lag stable at high number → consumer stuck on poison message → check DLT

question: Un consumidor falla al procesar un mensaje porque el payload tiene un formato inesperado. Tras 3 reintentos, ¿qué debería pasar?

options.label: A) Ignorar el mensaje y hacer commit del offset
options.description: Se pierde el evento pero el flujo no se detiene.

options.label: B) Enviar a un Dead Letter Topic (DLT) para inspección manual
options.description: Se publica en delivery-events.DLT con metadatos del error.

explanation: La opción B es el patrón de producción: el DLT permite no bloquear la partición ante un 'poison pill' (mensaje malformado) y permite investigar la causa raíz sin perder datos.

question: You have a topic "delivery-events" with 12 partitions. 500,000 deliveries/day. How do you choose partition keys? What are the trade-offs?
modelAnswer:
**Partition key = delivery ID (recommended for REWE transport):**

```java
kafka.send("delivery-events", delivery.getId().toString(), event);
// Same deliveryId → always same partition → ordered per delivery
```

**Why deliveryId:**
- All events for one delivery go to same partition → consumers see them IN ORDER
- PLANNED → DISPATCHED → IN_TRANSIT → DELIVERED (order preserved)
- Parallel processing across deliveries (different partitions)

**Trade-offs of different keys:**

| Key | Ordering guarantee | Risk |
|-----|-------------------|------|
| deliveryId | Per delivery | Balanced (500K unique IDs distribute well) |
| driverId | Per driver | Hot partitions (popular drivers get more events) |
| warehouseId | Per warehouse | Very unbalanced (3 warehouses = 3 hot partitions) |
| Random (null key) | None | Maximum throughput but no ordering |

**Sizing: 12 partitions for 500K/day:**
- ~6 events/second average, ~60/s peak
- 12 partitions can handle 12 parallel consumers
- Each partition: ~5 events/s peak → trivial for Kafka
- For REWE scale: 12 is fine. Would increase to 24-48 for 10M/day.

question: Instead of writing a custom consumer to sync delivery data to an analytics database, a colleague suggests Kafka Connect. When is Connect appropriate vs a custom consumer?
modelAnswer:
**Kafka Connect — use when:**
- Standard data flow: topic → database (or DB → topic)
- No complex business transformation needed
- Connector already exists (JDBC Sink, Elasticsearch Sink, S3 Sink)
- Want operational simplicity (config-only, no code)

**Custom consumer — use when:**
- Complex business logic during processing
- Need to call external APIs during consumption
- Custom error handling / retry strategies
- Multi-step transformations with state
- Need fine-grained control over commits/offsets

**REWE examples:**

Use Connect:
```
delivery-events → JDBC Sink Connector → analytics_db.delivery_events
driver-location → S3 Sink Connector → data lake (hourly batches)
```

Use custom consumer:
```
delivery-completed → BillingConsumer → validate + calculate price + create invoice + publish invoice-created
delivery-dispatched → NotificationConsumer → resolve template + call SMS API + handle retry
```

**Rule of thumb:** If the consumer does more than "take message, write to target" → custom consumer. If it's just data movement → Connect.

question: What problems do you see in this Kafka consumer?
problemsToIdentify: No idempotency — reprocessing creates duplicate invoices
No error handling — any exception kills the consumer (offset not committed)
Deserialization inline — failure on bad message blocks the partition
Too many responsibilities — invoice creation + email + analytics in one consumer
Synchronous email/analytics — slows consumption, couples to their availability
String payload — loses Avro/Schema Registry benefits, no schema validation

question: What is a consumer group rebalance? When does it happen? Why should you minimize it?
modelAnswer:
**Rebalance** = Kafka redistributes partitions among consumers in a group.

**When it happens:**
- Consumer joins the group (new pod scaled up)
- Consumer leaves (pod scaled down, crash, timeout)
- New partitions added to topic
- Consumer takes too long to poll (max.poll.interval.ms exceeded)

**During rebalance:**
- ALL consumers in the group STOP processing (stop-the-world)
- Partitions are reassigned
- Consumers resume from last committed offset

**Why minimize:**
- Processing stops during rebalance (seconds to minutes depending on group size)
- For REWE transport: no delivery events processed during this window
- Frequent rebalances = unstable system

**How to minimize:**
```properties
# Increase poll interval (if processing is slow)
max.poll.interval.ms=300000

# Reduce records per poll (process faster)
max.poll.records=100

# Session timeout (before broker considers consumer dead)
session.timeout.ms=30000

# Static group membership (Kafka 2.3+) — no rebalance on transient restart
group.instance.id=billing-consumer-pod-1
```

**Static membership**: Each consumer has a stable ID. If it restarts within session.timeout, its partitions are NOT reassigned — avoids unnecessary rebalance.

question: Should you publish one fine-grained event per field change (delivery-status-changed, delivery-driver-assigned, delivery-route-updated) or one coarse event (delivery-updated) with all fields?

options.label: A) Fine-grained: one event type per business action
options.description: DeliveryPlanned, DeliveryDispatched, DriverAssigned, RouteOptimized — each a separate event.

options.label: B) Coarse: one DeliveryUpdated event with before/after state
options.description: Single event type with full delivery state, consumers filter what they care about.

options.label: C) Hybrid: domain events for state transitions, data events for CRUD
options.description: Business actions → specific events. Administrative changes → generic updated event.

explanation:
Option A (fine-grained domain events) is preferred for logistics:

**Why:**
- Each event has clear business meaning (DeliveryDispatched = something happened)
- Consumers subscribe only to events they care about (filter by type)
- Event names document the domain language
- Consumers don't need to diff before/after to understand what changed
- Easier to build metrics: "count of DeliveryDispatched per hour"

**Event naming pattern:** `<Entity><PastTenseVerb>`
```
DeliveryPlanned
DeliveryDispatched
DeliveryCompleted
DeliveryFailed
DriverAssigned
RouteOptimized
```

**Why NOT B:** "DeliveryUpdated" says nothing about WHAT happened. Consumers must inspect the payload and compare fields to react. It's a disguised database change notification, not a domain event.

**When B is acceptable:** Low-importance CRUD operations, admin data, where no consumer needs specific behavior per change type.

question: Explain how Kafka transactions work and when you would use them at REWE.
Your colleague says "we should use event sourcing for deliveries." Is that the same as event-driven? Should you do it?
modelAnswer:
**They are DIFFERENT things:**

**Event-Driven Architecture (what REWE does):**
- Services communicate via events (Kafka)
- Events are notifications: "something happened"
- Each service has its OWN state/database
- Events are eventually consumed and processed
- Current state lives in the service's DB

**Event Sourcing (a persistence pattern):**
- Store ALL events as the source of truth (no current-state table)
- Current state is DERIVED by replaying events from the beginning
- Never update/delete — only append new events
- Must replay N events to reconstruct current state

**Should REWE use Event Sourcing for deliveries?**
Probably NOT, because:
- Delivery lifecycle is simple (5-6 states) → a status column + event table (audit) is sufficient
- Event sourcing adds complexity: projections, snapshots, eventual consistency
- Read patterns are simple: "what's the current status?" → single column query
- Team learning curve is steep

**When Event Sourcing makes sense:**
- Financial ledgers (every transaction is immutable, audit is the primary concern)
- Systems where "undo" is a core feature
- Complex state with many branching paths

**Recommendation:** Event-driven architecture + status column + audit trail (event table). Gets 90% of the benefit without the complexity of full event sourcing.

question: What producer settings are important for a reliable delivery-events producer?
modelAnswer:
**Critical producer settings:**

```properties
# Durability: wait for ALL replicas to acknowledge
acks=all

# Idempotent: no duplicate messages on retry
enable.idempotence=true

# Retries: handle transient broker failures
retries=2147483647  # effectively infinite (with delivery.timeout.ms as cap)
delivery.timeout.ms=120000  # 2 minutes total delivery attempt

# Ordering: one in-flight request ensures order per partition
max.in.flight.requests.per.connection=5  # safe with idempotence enabled

# Batching: efficiency (wait a bit to batch messages)
linger.ms=5  # wait 5ms for more messages to batch
batch.size=16384  # 16KB batch
```

**What these mean for REWE:**
- `acks=all`: a delivery event is not "sent" until all Kafka replicas have it — no data loss even if a broker dies
- `enable.idempotence=true`: if the producer retries (network timeout), Kafka deduplicates — no duplicate events
- Retries with timeout: transient failures auto-recover, eventually fails with clear error

**Error handling in producer:**
```java
kafka.send(topic, key, event).whenComplete((result, ex) -> {
    if (ex != null) {
        log.error("Failed to publish event after retries", ex);
        outboxRepo.save(new PendingEvent(event)); // fallback
    }
});
```

question: Should you have one topic per event type (delivery-planned, delivery-dispatched, delivery-completed) or one topic for all delivery events (delivery-events with type field)?

options.label: A) One topic per event type
options.description: delivery-planned, delivery-dispatched, delivery-completed, etc. Consumers subscribe to specific topics.

options.label: B) One topic for all delivery events (with type/header filtering)
options.description: delivery-events topic. Events have a "type" field. Consumers filter by type.

options.label: C) One topic per consumer (fan-out at producer)
options.description: billing-delivery-events, notification-delivery-events. Producer sends to multiple topics.

explanation:
Option B (single topic per aggregate/entity) is the recommended pattern:

**Why one topic for all delivery events:**
- **Ordering**: ALL events for a delivery are in the same partition (by deliveryId key)
- **Simplicity**: fewer topics to manage (ops)
- **Consumer flexibility**: billing can consume ALL delivery events, filter to "COMPLETED"
- **Correlation**: consumer sees the full lifecycle in order

**Why NOT A (topic per type):**
- Loses ordering across event types (dispatched might arrive before planned)
- Consumer that needs multiple types must subscribe to N topics
- Operationally: 10 event types × 5 entities = 50 topics to manage

**Why NOT C (topic per consumer):**
- Producer must know all consumers (tight coupling!)
- Adding a new consumer requires changing the producer
- Violates event-driven principle (producer shouldn't know consumers)

**Naming convention:**
```
{domain}-events     → delivery-events, driver-events, route-events
{domain}-commands   → delivery-commands (if using CQRS)
{domain}-events.DLT → dead letter for that topic
```

question: How do you test Kafka producers and consumers in Spring Boot?
When would you NOT use Kafka? What are the alternatives and when do they win?
modelAnswer:
**Kafka is NOT always the answer:**

| Scenario | Better choice | Why |
|----------|--------------|-----|
| Request-reply pattern | REST / gRPC | Kafka is fire-and-forget, not request-response |
| Small team, simple app | RabbitMQ | Simpler to operate, built-in routing, good enough for <10K msg/s |
| Strict ordering across ALL messages | Single-partition topic or DB queue | Kafka orders per partition only |
| Exactly-once to external system | Outbox + polling | Kafka can't transact with your DB |
| Temporary task queue | Redis Streams / SQS | Lightweight, no Zookeeper/KRaft overhead |
| Event bus within single JVM | CDI Events / Spring Events | No network hop, no serialization |

**Kafka wins when:**
- Multiple independent consumers need the same stream
- High throughput (>100K events/s)
- Replay/audit trail required
- Consumer speed varies (some fast, some slow)
- Event sourcing or stream processing (KStreams, ksqlDB)

**REWE context:**
- Kafka for inter-service events: ✓ (delivery, billing, analytics)
- Kafka for intra-service commands: ✗ (use direct method call)
- Kafka for simple notifications: maybe (RabbitMQ could also work, but Kafka is already there)

**Honest interview answer:** "I'd use Kafka when I need decoupled event streaming with multiple consumers and replay. For simpler messaging (one producer, one consumer, no replay), RabbitMQ or even a DB-backed queue might be simpler to operate."

question: What experience do you have with messaging?
answer:
I have strong production experience with JMS and ActiveMQ in enterprise systems.

I understand queues, topics, producers, consumers, asynchronous processing, retries, transactions and failure handling.

I have not used Kafka extensively in production yet, but I understand its core concepts and how it differs from traditional message brokers.

question: What is Kafka?
answer:
Kafka is a distributed event-streaming platform.

Producers publish records to topics.

Topics are divided into partitions, and records are stored durably and ordered within each partition.

Consumers read the records and track their progress using offsets.

Kafka is designed for high-throughput event streams, replay and multiple independent consumers.

question: What is the difference between ActiveMQ and Kafka?
answer:
ActiveMQ is a traditional message broker and works very well for queues, commands, request-reply messaging and enterprise JMS integration.

Kafka is based on a distributed append-only log and is optimised for durable event streams, high throughput, replay and multiple independent consumer groups.

ActiveMQ usually focuses on delivering a message to a consumer.

Kafka retains records for a configured period, allowing consumers to process or replay them independently.

question: What is a Kafka consumer group?
answer:
A consumer group is a set of consumers cooperating to process a topic.

Within one group, each partition is assigned to only one consumer at a time.

Different consumer groups can independently read the same topic.

For example, inventory, billing and analytics can each use a separate consumer group for the same order events.

question: What does idempotent consumer mean?
answer:
An idempotent consumer can process the same event more than once without creating an incorrect result.

This is important because distributed systems may redeliver messages.

For example, a payment event could include a unique event identifier, and the consumer stores processed identifiers to avoid charging the customer twice.

question: How would you handle Kafka processing failures?
answer:
I would use bounded retries with backoff for transient failures.

Consumers should be idempotent.

For messages that repeatedly fail, I would use a dead-letter topic or another controlled recovery mechanism.

I would monitor consumer lag, retry rates and dead-letter events.

The exact strategy depends on whether ordering must be preserved.

question: What happens during a Kafka consumer-group rebalance?
answer:
Kafka redistributes partitions among consumers in the same group.

During the rebalance, processing may pause, and partitions can move to different consumers. Long processing times or unstable consumers can cause frequent rebalances.

I would configure poll intervals carefully, keep handlers predictable and use cooperative rebalancing when appropriate.

question: How do you evolve an event schema without breaking consumers?
answer:
I prefer backward-compatible changes, such as adding optional fields with defaults.

Producers should not assume that all consumers are upgraded simultaneously. I would use schema validation, compatibility rules and version events only when the semantic contract truly changes.

question: How do you preserve ordering in Kafka?
answer:
Kafka guarantees ordering only within a partition.

Events that require ordering must use the same partition key, such as `deliveryId`. However, concentrating too many messages under one key can reduce parallelism, so the key must match the business ordering requirement.

question: Does exactly-once delivery mean the business operation happens exactly once?
answer:
Not automatically.

Kafka exactly-once features can coordinate Kafka reads and writes, but side effects in an external database or HTTP service still require idempotency or another consistency mechanism.

I design consumers assuming that duplicate delivery can occur.

mission: You have replication.factor=3, min.insync.replicas=2, acks=all. Broker 3 goes down. What happens to produce requests?
choices: Produce succeeds but with acks=1 fallback
answer: Produce succeeds (2 ISR remaining >= min.insync.replicas)
explain: With acks=all, the producer waits for ALL in-sync replicas to acknowledge. ISR shrinks to 2 (leader + 1 follower). Since min.insync.replicas=2 and we have 2 ISR members, produce succeeds. If ANOTHER broker goes down (ISR=1 < min.insync.replicas=2), produce would fail with NotEnoughReplicasException.

mission: acks=all, min.insync.replicas=2, replication.factor=3. ALL brokers go down. Broker 3 (which was OUT of sync) comes back first. What happens?
choices: Broker 3 becomes leader, some messages lost (unclean election)
Partition stays unavailable until an ISR member returns
Broker 3 becomes leader with no data loss
Kafka automatically recovers all messages from disk
answer: Partition stays unavailable until an ISR member returns
explain: With unclean.leader.election.enable=false (default since 0.11), Kafka will NOT promote an out-of-sync replica. The partition remains unavailable until a broker that was in the ISR recovers. This preserves data integrity at the cost of availability. With unclean.leader.election.enable=true, Broker 3 would become leader but messages not replicated to it would be LOST.

mission: Producer sends message with acks=1. Leader writes to log and responds SUCCESS. Leader crashes before followers replicate. What happens?
choices: Message is safe — followers will get it eventually
Message is LOST — new leader never received it
Producer gets an exception and can retry
answer: Message is LOST — new leader never received it
explain: With acks=1, the producer only waits for the leader to acknowledge. If the leader crashes before followers replicate, the message exists only on the crashed leader's disk. The new leader (elected from ISR) never received it. When the old leader recovers, it truncates its log to match the new leader. The message is permanently lost. Solution: use acks=all.

question: How do you achieve exactly-once delivery end-to-end with Kafka?
interviewerIntent: Tests deep understanding of delivery guarantees across producer, broker, and consumer
keyPoints: Idempotent producer: prevents duplicates from retries (per partition)
Transactions: atomic writes across multiple partitions
Consumer: read_committed isolation + idempotent processing
End-to-end: idempotent producer + transactional consumer (read-process-write pattern)
OR: consumer-side dedup (INSERT ON CONFLICT, check offset)

followUps.question: What is the performance cost of exactly-once?
followUps.answerHint: Transactions add ~3-5% latency. Idempotent producer is nearly free (sequence numbers are lightweight).

followUps.question: When is at-least-once + consumer idempotency better than transactions?
followUps.answerHint: When writing to external systems (databases, APIs) that dont support Kafka transactions. Most real-world cases.

explain: True exactly-once requires: (1) Idempotent producer (no duplicates), (2) Transactions for atomic multi-partition writes, (3) Consumer with read_committed + idempotent sink. In practice, most systems use at-least-once + idempotent consumer (INSERT ON CONFLICT) because it is simpler and works with external systems.

mission: What happens when a broker crashes? Order the recovery steps:

items.label: Controller detects broker failure (ZK ephemeral node disappears)
Controller elects new leaders from ISR for affected partitions
Controller sends LeaderAndISR requests to remaining brokers
New leaders start serving produce/consume requests
Crashed broker recovers and rejoins as follower
Recovered broker replicates missed messages from new leaders

explain: The controller handles all leader elections. Clients experience a brief timeout (~ms to seconds) then transparently connect to new leaders. The recovered broker catches up by replicating from current leaders before rejoining the ISR.

question: What is log compaction and when would you use it?
interviewerIntent: Tests understanding of Kafka as more than a queue — also a state store
keyPoints: Keeps only LATEST value per key (vs time-based retention)
Old values for same key are removed during compaction
Tombstone (null value) = delete key entirely after compaction
Use cases: current state per entity, CDC, KTable materialization
NOT for event sourcing (need full history) or time-windowed analytics

followUps.question: How is this different from a database?
followUps.answerHint: Its still append-only and immutable until compaction runs. Consumers still read a log, not query by key. For key-value lookups, use KTable or materialize to a DB.

followUps.question: Can you have both retention AND compaction?
followUps.answerHint: Yes: cleanup.policy=compact,delete. Compact keeps latest per key, delete removes segments older than retention period.

explain: Log compaction transforms a topic from "complete history" to "latest state per key". Like a changelog where only the most recent entry per entity survives. Used for driver-status topics, configuration propagation, and CDC replication.

mission: replication.factor=3. A follower hasn't fetched for 15 seconds (replica.lag.time.max.ms=10000). What happens?
choices: Follower is permanently removed from cluster
answer: Follower is removed from ISR
explain: After replica.lag.time.max.ms (10s) without a fetch, the follower is removed from ISR. It can rejoin once it catches up. This doesn't affect writes (as long as ISR >= min.insync.replicas). The follower is still a replica, just not in-sync.

mission: Producer config: acks=all, enable.idempotence=true. Network glitch causes timeout. Producer retries. What happens at the broker?
choices: Broker detects duplicate via sequence number, discards it
Exception thrown at producer, no retry
Broker stores both but marks one as duplicate
answer: Broker detects duplicate via sequence number, discards it
explain: Idempotent producer assigns a sequence number per message per partition. On retry, the broker sees the same ProducerID + sequence number and silently discards the duplicate. The producer gets a success acknowledgment. No duplicates in the log.

mission: batch.size=16384, linger.ms=100. Producer sends 1 small message (200 bytes). How long before it's sent to broker?
choices: After 100ms OR when batch is full, whichever comes first
answer: After 100ms OR when batch is full, whichever comes first
explain: The producer sends a batch when EITHER condition is met: batch.size bytes accumulated OR linger.ms elapsed since first message was added to batch. With only 200 bytes, the batch won't fill, so it waits the full 100ms. If more messages arrive within 100ms filling the batch, it sends earlier.

mission: Topic delivery-status has cleanup.policy=compact. You send: key=D-1 value=PLANNED, then key=D-1 value=DELIVERED. After compaction, how many records exist for D-1?
choices: 2 (both kept, compaction only removes by time)
answer: 1 (only value=DELIVERED survives)
explain: Log compaction keeps only the LATEST value per key. After compaction runs, only the record with value=DELIVERED (higher offset) survives for key D-1. The PLANNED record is removed. This is different from time-based retention which removes entire segments regardless of keys.

mission: You send key=D-1 value=null to a compacted topic. What does this mean?
choices: Tombstone: key D-1 will be removed after compaction
answer: Tombstone: key D-1 will be removed after compaction
explain: A null value is called a tombstone. It marks the key for deletion. After compaction (and a configurable delay: delete.retention.ms), the key and tombstone are both removed. Not immediate — consumers have time to see the tombstone and act on it (e.g., delete from local store).

mission: Topic has 6 partitions. Consumer group has 8 consumers. How many consumers are idle?
answer: 2 (6 partitions, max 6 active consumers)
explain: Each partition is assigned to exactly ONE consumer in a group. With 6 partitions and 8 consumers, 6 get one partition each and 2 sit idle. They serve as standby — if an active consumer fails, an idle one takes over during rebalance. To use all 8, increase partitions to >= 8.

question: What causes a consumer group rebalance, and how do you minimize its impact?
interviewerIntent: Tests operational understanding of consumer group coordination
keyPoints: Triggers: consumer joins, leaves, crashes, subscription change, partition count change
During rebalance: ALL consumers stop processing (stop-the-world in eager protocol)
Cooperative rebalance (incremental): only affected partitions reassigned, others continue
session.timeout.ms (10s) and heartbeat.interval.ms (3s) control detection speed
max.poll.interval.ms (5min): if processing takes longer, consumer is considered dead

followUps.question: How does static group membership help?
followUps.answerHint: group.instance.id makes consumer sticky. On brief disconnect, its partitions wait instead of immediate rebalance. Reduces rebalance during rolling restarts.

followUps.question: What is the danger of max.poll.interval.ms being too low?
followUps.answerHint: If processing a batch takes longer than max.poll.interval, consumer is kicked. Causes rebalance which assigns same partitions to another consumer. Can create duplicate processing.

explain: Rebalances are Kafka's mechanism for redistributing partitions. They pause consumption. Minimize by: using cooperative protocol, setting appropriate timeouts, using static membership for stable consumers, and ensuring processing time < max.poll.interval.ms.

mission: Producer sends 3 messages to a topic with 3 partitions. Keys: null, null, null. How are they distributed?
answer: Round-robin: one per partition
explain: With null key, Kafka's default partitioner uses round-robin (sticky partitioner in newer versions batches to same partition then rotates). Messages are evenly distributed for maximum parallelism. No ordering guarantee since they're on different partitions.

mission: Consumer reads offset 50, processes the message, but crashes BEFORE committing offset. What happens on restart?
choices: Starts from offset 50 (last committed was 49) — message reprocessed
answer: Starts from offset 50 (last committed was 49) — message reprocessed
explain: Kafka tracks committed offsets per consumer group. If offset 50 was processed but not committed, the consumer restarts from the last committed offset (49+1=50). The message is reprocessed — this is why Kafka is 'at-least-once' by default. Consumer must be idempotent.
